EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Gulf of Alaska low moved to Prince William Sound producing snow over the Chugach Mountains along with strong wind. The combination of the wind and snow resulted in blizzard conditions from Keystone Canyon through Thompson Pass December 10th. The Thompson Pass DOT crew reported around 1.5 feet of snow in the pass during this event.

A strong Gulf of Alaska storm coupled with deep cold arctic air over interior Alaska produced strong north gap winds through the Chugach Mountains. Peak wind gusts of 94 mph were observed at the Valdez airport during this event.
A strong storm in the Gulf of Alaska produced strong northwest wind across the Alaska Peninsula.
A strong low moved along the Aleutians and Alaska Peninsula producing strong northeast wind with snow and blowing snow resulting in a blizzard.
A strong storm moved through the eastern Bering Sea producing snow and wind that resulted in blizzard conditions in the Pribilof Islands.
A storm moved into south central Alaska Friday, December 31st that produced strong wind and snow through the Thompson Pass area resulting in a blizzard.

A storm over southwest Alaska produced strong moist southerly flow into the Susitna Valley. This resulted in moderate to heavy snowfall, especially in the northern and western portions of the Susitna Valley. Snow fall amounts ranged from 6 inches in some areas to 18 inches on the East Fork and 17 inches at Eaglesong Lodge.

An intense Bering Sea storm pushed a front inland along the Bering Sea coast the evening of December 2nd. A low formed along the front in the northern Gulf of Alaska late in the afternoon of December 3rd. Strong wind combined with snow produced blizzard conditions across portions of southwest that then spread into the Cook Inlet region and Prince William Sound. Heavy snow fell in the Susitna Valley in the strong moist southerly flow Friday and Saturday.

A major winter storm had explosive development well south of Kodiak on Halloween. The deepest that the storm got was 940 MB. On 11/1, a very strong front associated with this storm moved onto the coast of SE Alaska. Warm moist air moving over colder air in the northern Lynn Canal area caused heavy snow especially for higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Skagway coop observed 0.86 inch of rainfall on 11/1. Frazier Camp on the Canadian side of the Klondike Highway was below freezing the entire time. White Pass has an elevation of 3116 FT. It appears upon analysis and given the elevation of White Pass, heavy snow fell there on 11/1, but the were no official reports from DOT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm center developed just off the Queen Charlotte Islands on the afternoon of 10/9. The center recurved to the NW over the Panhandle causing hurricane force winds, with many, many power outages and downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although there were no land observations available for this wind storm, hurricane force NE wind was detected at Cape Spenser at 1400 AKST on 10/9.

Wind to 72 MPH was observed with this system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderate strength front stalled just off the coast on the evening of 10/28. Moist air was forced up Lynn Canal which caused heavy snow overnight in White Pass. 6 to 8 inches of new snow fell there in 12 hours on that night into the early morning of 10/29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Along the Klondike Highway up to White Pass, 6 to 8 inches of new snow fell between the hours of 2100 AKST 10/28 and 0700 10/29. Alaska DOT also reported winds to 30 MPH with very poor visibility.

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A massive storm force low complex developed over the Gulf of Alaska on 2/28 and persisted into 3/2. A strong front moved onto the west coast of Prince of Wales Island on the morning of 3/1 bringing strong winds that diminished around noon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds occurred for the western part of Prince of Wales Island on the morning of 3/1. The strongest recorded wind was at Hydaburg AWOS with a gust of 55 KT at 0416 AKST on 3/1.

CZ_NAME: CAPE DECISION TO SALISBURY SOUND COASTAL AREA

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sitka Airport had wind gusts to 50 MPH at 0300 AKST on 3/28. NCEP analyses showed very strong surface pressure gradients along  the west coast of Baranof Island during the early morning of 3/28 and headland winds exceeded 60 MPH.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although Lincoln Rock is a windy marine observation, the extreme winds observed there on 3/27 indicated that it was very windy for land areas. At 1700 AKST on 3/27 Lincoln Rock measured a gust to 85 KT. There were many readings in the 70s and lower 80s. Winds in Ketchikan were up to 48 MPH. Hydaburg near to the area had a peak wind of 71 MPH. There were no reports of significant damage, but minor power outages.

High freezing levels combined with moderate rain in the Susitna Valley resulted in the Yentna River rising above flood stage August 5th. Rainfall in the Susitna Valley were 1 to 1.5 inches prior to the rising water.

An intense 962 MB low tracked through the Gulf of Alaska from the south and then over the Barren Islands region before weakening. A strong front associated with this low brought warning level winds to both public and marine areas. The areas with highest recorded winds were Portage with wind gusts over 60 MPH from 7 AM ADT Oct 1, 2010 to 10 AM ADT Oct 2, 2010. The peak wind in Portage was 78 MPH. Whittier also had winds gusts over 60 mph from 9 AM ADT Oct 1 through 12 PM Oct 2. Whittier had a peak gust of 70 MPH at 6 AM ADT Oct 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted over 60 MPH in Whittier and Portage from the morning of October 1st through the morning of October 2nd. Peak wind was 78 MPH in Portage and 70 MPH in Whittier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of lows brought steady and moderate rain across the Kenai peninsula and contributed to the flooding of the Resurrection River. Light rain began in Seward on Sept 29 and moderate rain began by the afternoon. Light rain continued through Sept 30, with moderate and even heavy rain Oct 1 and Oct 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall along the Eastern Kenai Mountains caused the Resurrection River near Seward, Alaska to reach flood stage. It was over flood stage from Oct 2 at 7 AM ADT through 4 PM ADT Oct 2nd. The crest was at 18.17 ft, which is .67 ft over flood stage.|In addition, the Seward Emergency Manager reported a mudslide on mile 19 with only one lane open.  Water was up to the road at Salmon Creek road and Nash road.  There was also an unconfirmed report of water over the bridge near the prison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low and associated front briefly produced strong wind in the Dutch Harbor area the evening of October 15th.

A strong storm in the Gulf of Alaska coupled with high pressure and cold air in interior Alaska produced strong outflow gap wind through the Chugach Mountains. Recent snow in the Chugach Mountains was suspended in the intense north wind through the Thompson Pass. The peak gust for this event was measured at 108 KT at the Thompson Pass RWIS. The Copper River Delta does not have observation equipment, therefore the wind for that region is estimated and assumed to have reached at least 80 mph based upon the observed pressure gradient between Glennallen and Cordova and the observed wind in Thompson Pass.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based upon Strawberry Reef observations and rule of thumb to double the observed peak gust as an estimate of wind in the main wind channel of the Copper River Delta.

Peak wind gust of 114 mph were measured in Thompson Pass and record high wind gusts of 94 and 97 mph were measured at the Valdez airport during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RWIS site at Thompson Pass measured the peak gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A classic Prince William Sound low pumped moisture from the Gulf of Alaska across the Kenai Peninsula resulting in heavy banded snowfall across the central to northern Kenai Peninsula. Snowfall amounts ranged from a few inches along the periphery of the heaviest band to as much as 28 inches in interior locations in the Kenai Mountains at Cooper Landing and around 28 inches in the community of Girdwood.

A 996 mb low near Banks Island combined with a 1037 mb high across the southern Chukchi Sea to produce a period of blizzard conditions along the Beaufort Sea Coast.  Severe blizzard conditions were observed along the eastern Beaufort Sea Coast.  At Barter Island, the wind gusted to at least 63kt/72 mph and then power was lost at the AWSS. According to reports from the public in the area, the power went out for about 4 hours on the morning of the 2nd, and the visibility was reduced to zero at times for about 1 1/2 days.  Once the storm ended there were snowdrifts that were estimated between 10 and 12 feet high.||Along the Central Beaufort Sea coast, conditions were not as severe, but blizzard conditions were observed from the early morning hours on the 1st through the mid-morning hours on the 2nd.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow.  The wind gusted as high as 42kt/48 mph at the Deadhorse ASOS.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hickerson Lake RAWS report 140 mph gusts. This RAWS is over 1000 feet in elevation. Based upon this observation and observations around Kodiak Island, it is estimated wind gusts were around 80 mph in some of the populated areas near sea level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary associated with a 984 mb low near Wrangel Island produced heavy snowfall and blizzard conditions along portions of the west coast of Alaska on December 18-19, 2010.||Zone 207: Blizzard conditions were observed at the Kivalina ASOS from 1753AKST on the 18th through 0330AKST on the 19th.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter of a mile in snow and blowing snow.  A peak wind gust of 42 kt (48 mph) was observed during this event.  At Point Hope near-blizzard conditions were observed at times, but the visibility was one half mile or better during much of this event. The wind at the Point Hope AWOS did gust as high as 44 kt (51 mph).||Zone 208: Blizzard conditions were observed at the Red Dog Mine from 0806AKST through 1331AKST on the 19th.  The visibility was frequently one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow.  A peak wind gust of 35 kt (40 mph) was observed at the Red Dog Mine.||Zone 209: Blizzard conditions were observed at Kotzebue from 1353AKST on the 18th through 1553AKST on the 19th.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow. A peak wind gust of 47 kt (54 mph) was observed at the Kotzebue ASOS.  Near-blizzard conditions were also observed at Selawik from 0453AKST through 1516AKST on the 19th. A peak wind gust of 32 kt (37 mph) was observed at the Selawik AWOS. Although the visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter of a mile the wind was not as strong inland as it was along the coast.||Zone 211: Heavy snow was observed at Nome from late on the morning of the 18th through the late morning on the 19th. A storm total of 10 inches was observed at the Nome National Weather Service office.||Zone 213: Peak wind gusts of 53 kt (61 mph) was observed at Both the Teller and Wales AWSS's during the late morning and early afternoon hours on the 18th.  The high winds were accompanied by falling snow and the visibility was briefly reduced to one quarter mile in snow and blowing snow at Wales, but the blizzard conditions were short lived and the visibility was one half mile or better during much of this event.  It is likely that blizzard conditions occurred in spots along the Bering Strait Coast, but there were no reports.||Zone 214: Blizzard conditions were observed at Scammon Bay from 1325AKST through 1625AKST on the 18th.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow.  The wind gusted as high as 46 kt (53 mph) at the Scammon Bay AWSS.

A strong ridge of high pressure across eastern Russia combined with low pressure in the Gulf of Alaska to produce a strong pressure difference along the Chukchi Sea coast, the Bering Strait coast and on Saint Lawrence Island.  The strong difference in pressure produced strong winds and created significant blowing and drifting snow and blizzard conditions.||Zone 207: Blizzard conditions were observed at Kivalina from 1800AKST on the 25th through 0600AKST on the 26th.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less with near zero visibility at times in blowing snow.  A peak wind gust of 52 Kt (60 mph) was observed at the Kivalina ASOS. ||Zone 213: Blizzard conditions were observed at Wales along the Bering Strait Coast and at Gambell on Saint Lawrence Island.  At Wales, the blizzard conditions were observed from approximately 0300AKST on the 25th through 0400AKST on the 26th.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow.  A peak wind gust of 48 kt (55 mph) was observed at the Wales AWIS.  Blizzard conditions were also observed at Gambell beginning at around 0300AKST on the 25th, however, the visibility sensor became inoperable during the event and it is estimated that the blizzard conditions continued through around 1200AKST on the 26th.  A peak wind gust of 46 kt (53 mph) was observed at the Gambell AWOS.  Blizzard conditions likely also occurred at Savoonga; however, the visibility sensor was inoperable during the event. A peak wind gust of 50 kt (58 mph) was observed at the Savoonga AWOS.

A strong ridge of high pressure across eastern Russia combined with low pressure in the Gulf of Alaska to produce a strong winds and blizzard conditions at Savoonga on the 25th into the 26th (see separate episode on the blizzard). Once the blizzard conditions ended a north wind of 25 to 40 mph continued on the 27th.  The electric lines and insulators became laden with frozen salt water from the Bering Sea.  The strong winds caused arcing that caused the power lines to short out, and the power remained out for about three days.  One hundred forty people took shelter at the school during this time. Many pipes in the village were frozen and burst during the outage.  Of the 159 homes at Savoonga, 39 sustained damage due to water pipes freezing and bursting from the lack of heat once the homes lost electricity to their furnaces, boilers and water pumps. The city water system remained online.

Temperatures near 30 below combined with a west wind of around 20 mph at Barter Island to produce wind chills as low as 61 below from approximately 0400AKST through 0800AKST on the 28th. Although the 60 below wind chills were observed for only about 4 hours, the wind chills the remainder of the day on the 28th were mainly in the 55 to 60 below range. At Deadhorse, temperatures of 40 below combined with a southwest wind of 15 mph to produce wind chills as low as 75 below from approximately 0700AKST until 1700AKST on the 29th.

An extremely warm and moist air mass moving around a large ridge of high pressure in the north Pacific produced a prolonged period of freezing rain across much of interior Alaska on November 22-24, 2010.  ||Zone 216: A mix of freezing rain and snow fell across the Middle Yukon and Lower Koyukuk Valleys.  Temperatures were near freezing during the event and in spots the precipitation changed back and forth between freezing rain and snow. A total of 72 hundredths of an inch of rain and melted snow was observed at the Kaltag ASOS with 29 hundredths at Ruby AWSS. A two day total of 99 hundredths of an inch was observed at the Galena Coop, which included 2.5 inches of snow on the 23rd. It is likely that some areas had between one quarter and one half of an inch of ice accumulation on untreated surfaces. ||Zone 217: Based on the observations from Ambler and Shungnak it is likely that freezing rain fell in many areas south of the Kobuk River.  The Selawik RAWS observed a total of 47 hundredths of an inch of precipitation and temperatures were near or a little above freezing during the event. ||Zone 219: A mix of freezing rain and snow fell across The Upper Koyukuk Valley.  |The precipitation fell in the form of snow at Bettles with a storm total of 3 inches. The precipitation likely started as freezing rain in the valleys south of Bettles as well as across the higher elevations along the Dalton Highway where the Gobblers Knob Snotel observered 2 to 3 tenths of an inch of precipitation, which likely fell at least in part in the form of freezing rain.  ||Zone 220: A mix of freezing rain and snow was observed at Central, Circle and Fort Yukon.  Three day totals of just under one half inch of liquid precipitation were observed at the above locations.  The Steese Highway was likely very treacherous in spots from Fox to Circle. ||Zone 221: A total of 1.52 inches of rain was observed at the Nenana ASOS and 99 hundredths of an inch was observed at the Tanana ASOS.  A total of 1.63 inches was observed at Lake Minchumina.  Temperatures were within a few degrees of freezing during the duration of the storm and this led to a significant build up of ice on roads in areas such as Nenana and Tanana.  The rain did mix with and change over to snow in spots, especially from Manley Hot Springs west. The DOT had a travel advisory out for the Elliot Highway from Wickersham Dome (MP 28) to Manley Hot Springs (MP 153) due to very slick conditions caused by freezing rain and snow. ||Zone 222: A total of 95 hundredths of an inch of rain fell at the Fairbanks International Airport from 0500AKST on the 22nd through 0327AKST on November 24th.  The rainfall on the 22nd of one half inch was a daily record, and the 32 hundredths of an inch of rain that fell on the 23rd was also a new daily record.  This storm produced the second greatest winter rainfall of record at Fairbanks and the greatest ever observed during the month of November.  Some locations in the Fairbanks area observed over an inch of rain, with 1.37 inches at Keystone Ridge on Murphy Dome and 1.16 inches at Ester.  Temperatures during the event were near to or a little above freezing.  In fact, there was a period of 49 consecutive hours with temperatures above freezing at the Fairbanks International Airport.  This was the longest stretch of above freezing temperatures ever observed in Fairbanks during the month of November.  The rain froze on colder surfaces and made the roads extremely hazardous with up to 1 inch of ice accumulation on many roads.  Travel was nearly impossible in spots on the 22nd into the 23rd.  The icy roads led to numerous traffic accidents with many vehicles in ditches around the area.  Although the ice did accumulate in spots on trees and power lines, the buildup of ice was not significant and resulted in only sporadic power outages that in most areas lasted for no more than 12 hours.  The ice storm was one of the worst in memory and shut down schools, the University of Alaska, and government offices at the Federal, State, Borough and City level. Many small businesses closed for a good part of this time.  Although Fairbanks International Airport remained open there were some flight cancellations on the 22nd and 23rd.  ||Zone 223: Freezing rain fell at Delta Junction from 0923AKST on the 22nd until 1307AKST on the 23rd.  A total of 28 hundredths of an inch of rain was observed at the Delta Junction ASOS. Fifty-nine hundreths of an inch of rain was reported at the Whitestone Farm Co-op Observation site. The freezing rain caused the school and city offices to shut down as well as Fort Greely.||Zone 224: Freezing rain fell on the night of the 23rd through early morning on the 24th. One-tenth of an inch of rain was reported by a Co-op observer and also by a Storm Spotter.||Zone 225: Freezing rain fell at Healy as well as at Denali National Park visitors center from the late morning hours on the 22nd through the early morning hours on the 24th.  A total of 6 tenths of an inch of rain was observed at the Denali Visitors Center Raws with 42 hundredths of an inch observed at Healy. According to reports from DOT the Parks Highway had about an inch of ice from MP 260 north (about 10 miles north of Healy) to the Fairbanks area.||Zone 226: Freezing rain fell across parts of the Eastern Alaska Range from the early afternoon hours on the 23rd through the morning hours on the 24th.  A trained weather spotter in Tok observed one tenth of an inch of ice at 5 am on the 24th.  Another trained weather observer at the Robertson River Bridge reported that freezing rain began a little after midnight on the 24th and that the Alaska Highway was likely treacherous based on the amount of ice that was observed at the Robertson River Bridge.||Zone 227: A total of 2.08 inches of rain fell at McGrath and 2.86 inches was observed at Telida with 62 hundredths of an inch at Nikolai.  At the start of the event temperatures were below freezing as the rain fell and produced a quarter inch of ice on untreated surfaces at McGrath.  Temperatures warmed well above freezing during the afternoon hours on the 22nd and the freezing rain changed over to rain in most areas.  The rain fell on top of the ice that had accumulated and continued to produce very slick surfaces.

A 1008 mb low just offshore of Barter Island combined with a 1036 mb high in the Chukchi Sea to produce blizzard conditions along the eastern Beaufort Sea Coast from approximately 1800AKST on the 20th through 1200AKST on the 22nd.  According to reports from law enforcement the visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less with white-out conditions at times.  According to reports from both law enforcement and the public the blizzard was not as severe as the one that occurred on December 14th-15th. A peak wind gust of 53 kt (61 mph) was observed at the Barter Island AWSS.

A 1009 mb low in the southern Chukchi Sea combined with a 1038 mb high across the northern interior to produce a period of blizzard conditions at Kivalina during the morning hours on the 23rd.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile in snow and blowing snow from 0500AKST through 1100AKST. A peak wind gust of 39 kt (45 mph) was observed at the Kivalina ASOS.  Blizzard conditions were also observed at times during the morning at Point Hope where a peak wind gust of 46 kt (53 mph) was observed at the Point Hope AWOS.

An extremely warm and moist air mass moving around a large ridge of high pressure in the north Pacific produced a prolonged period of freezing rain across much of interior Alaska on November 22-24, 2010.  ||Zone 216: A mix of freezing rain and snow fell across the Middle Yukon and Lower Koyukuk Valleys.  Temperatures were near freezing during the event and in spots the precipitation changed back and forth between freezing rain and snow. A total of 72 hundredths of an inch of rain and melted snow was observed at the Kaltag ASOS with 29 hundredths at Ruby AWSS. A two day total of 99 hundredths of an inch was observed at the Galena Coop, which included 2.5 inches of snow on the 23rd. It is likely that some areas had between one quarter and one half of an inch of ice accumulation on untreated surfaces. ||Zone 217: Based on the observations from Ambler and Shungnak it is likely that freezing rain fell in many areas south of the Kobuk River.  The Selawik RAWS observed a total of 47 hundredths of an inch of precipitation and temperatures were near or a little above freezing during the event. ||Zone 219: A mix of freezing rain and snow fell across The Upper Koyukuk Valley.  |The precipitation fell in the form of snow at Bettles with a storm total of 3 inches. The precipitation likely started as freezing rain in the valleys south of Bettles as well as across the higher elevations along the Dalton Highway where the Gobblers Knob Snotel observered 2 to 3 tenths of an inch of precipitation, which likely fell at least in part in the form of freezing rain.  ||Zone 220: A mix of freezing rain and snow was observed at Central, Circle and Fort Yukon.  Three day totals of just under one half inch of liquid precipitation were observed at the above locations.  The Steese Highway was likely very treacherous in spots from Fox to Circle. ||Zone 221: A total of 1.52 inches of rain was observed at the Nenana ASOS and 99 hundredths of an inch was observed at the Tanana ASOS.  A total of 1.63 inches was observed at Lake Minchumina.  Temperatures were within a few degrees of freezing during the duration of the storm and this led to a significant build up of ice on roads in areas such as Nenana and Tanana.  The rain did mix with and change over to snow in spots, especially from Manley Hot Springs west. The DOT had a travel advisory out for the Elliot Highway from Wickersham Dome (MP 28) to Manley Hot Springs (MP 153) due to very slick conditions caused by freezing rain and snow. ||Zone 222: A total of 95 hundredths of an inch of rain fell at the Fairbanks International Airport from 0500AKST on the 22nd through 0327AKST on November 24th.  The rainfall on the 22nd of one half inch was a daily record, and the 32 hundredths of an inch of rain that fell on the 23rd was also a new daily record.  This storm produced the second greatest winter rainfall of record at Fairbanks and the greatest ever observed during the month of November.  Some locations in the Fairbanks area observed over an inch of rain, with 1.37 inches at Keystone Ridge on Murphy Dome and 1.16 inches at Ester.  Temperatures during the event were near to or a little above freezing.  In fact, there was a period of 49 consecutive hours with temperatures above freezing at the Fairbanks International Airport.  This was the longest stretch of above freezing temperatures ever observed in Fairbanks during the month of November.  The rain froze on colder surfaces and made the roads extremely hazardous with up to 1 inch of ice accumulation on many roads.  Travel was nearly impossible in spots on the 22nd into the 23rd.  The icy roads led to numerous traffic accidents with many vehicles in ditches around the area.  Although the ice did accumulate in spots on trees and power lines, the buildup of ice was not significant and resulted in only sporadic power outages that in most areas lasted for no more than 12 hours.  The ice storm was one of the worst in memory and shut down schools, the University of Alaska, and government offices at the Federal, State, Borough and City level. Many small businesses closed for a good part of this time.  Although Fairbanks International Airport remained open there were some flight cancellations on the 22nd and 23rd.  ||Zone 223: Freezing rain fell at Delta Junction from 0923AKST on the 22nd until 1307AKST on the 23rd.  A total of 28 hundredths of an inch of rain was observed at the Delta Junction ASOS. Fifty-nine hundreths of an inch of rain was reported at the Whitestone Farm Co-op Observation site. The freezing rain caused the school and city offices to shut down as well as Fort Greely.||Zone 224: Freezing rain fell on the night of the 23rd through early morning on the 24th. One-tenth of an inch of rain was reported by a Co-op observer and also by a Storm Spotter.||Zone 225: Freezing rain fell at Healy as well as at Denali National Park visitors center from the late morning hours on the 22nd through the early morning hours on the 24th.  A total of 6 tenths of an inch of rain was observed at the Denali Visitors Center Raws with 42 hundredths of an inch observed at Healy. According to reports from DOT the Parks Highway had about an inch of ice from MP 260 north (about 10 miles north of Healy) to the Fairbanks area.||Zone 226: Freezing rain fell across parts of the Eastern Alaska Range from the early afternoon hours on the 23rd through the morning hours on the 24th.  A trained weather spotter in Tok observed one tenth of an inch of ice at 5 am on the 24th.  Another trained weather observer at the Robertson River Bridge reported that freezing rain began a little after midnight on the 24th and that the Alaska Highway was likely treacherous based on the amount of ice that was observed at the Robertson River Bridge.||Zone 227: A total of 2.08 inches of rain fell at McGrath and 2.86 inches was observed at Telida with 62 hundredths of an inch at Nikolai.  At the start of the event temperatures were below freezing as the rain fell and produced a quarter inch of ice on untreated surfaces at McGrath.  Temperatures warmed well above freezing during the afternoon hours on the 22nd and the freezing rain changed over to rain in most areas.  The rain fell on top of the ice that had accumulated and continued to produce very slick surfaces.

Some locations in the Fairbanks area observed over an inch of rain, with 1.37 inches at Keystone Ridge on Murphy Dome and 1.16 inches at Ester.  Temperatures during the event were near to or a little above freezing.  In fact, there was a period of 49 consecutive hours with temperatures above freezing at the Fairbanks International Airport.  This was the longest stretch of above freezing temperatures ever observed in Fairbanks during the month of November.  The rain froze on colder surfaces and made the roads extremely hazardous with up to 1 inch of ice accumulation on many roads.  Travel was nearly impossible in spots on the 22nd into the 23rd.  The icy roads led to numerous traffic accidents with many vehicles in ditches around the area.  Although the ice did accumulate in spots on trees and power lines, the buildup of ice was not significant and resulted in only sporadic power outages that in most areas lasted for no more than 12 hours.  The ice storm was one of the worst in memory and shut down schools, the University of Alaska, and government offices at the Federal, State, Borough and City level. Many small businesses closed for a good part of this time.  Although Fairbanks International Airport remained open there were some flight cancellations on the 22nd and 23rd.  ||Zone 223: Freezing rain fell at Delta Junction from 0923AKST on the 22nd until 1307AKST on the 23rd.  A total of 28 hundredths of an inch of rain was observed at the Delta Junction ASOS. Fifty-nine hundreths of an inch of rain was reported at the Whitestone Farm Co-op Observation site. The freezing rain caused the school and city offices to shut down as well as Fort Greely.||Zone 224: Freezing rain fell on the night of the 23rd through early morning on the 24th. The combination of a 1053 mb high centered a few hundred miles north of Barrow and a 987 mb low in the western Gulf of Alaska produced northeast winds of 15 to 30 mph along the western Arctic Coast.  The wind combined with temperatures of 30 to 35 below to produce wind chills as low as 70 below at Point Lay, and 62 below at the Atqasuk AWSS.  The wind chills at Point Lay were estimated based on the temperature and strength of the pressure gradient as the anemometer at the Point Lay AWOS was inoperable during this event.

A strong pressure gradient between a 975 mb low across the Kamchatka Peninsula and a 1030 mb high across northeast Alaska produced a period of blizzard conditions on Saint Lawrence Island from around 4 am AKST through 6 pm AKST on the 22nd. The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile, and occasionally to near zero with white-out conditions.  A peak wind gust of 55kt/63 mph was observed at the Savoonga AWOS.

A 1054 mb high off the arctic coast combined with areas of low pressure in the Gulf of Alaska and eastern Bering Sea to produce a strong pressure gradient along the Beaufort Sea Coast. A northeast wind of 25 to 40 mph along the central Beaufort Sea Coast, and 35 to 50 mph along the eastern Beaufort Sea coast produced significant blowing and drifting snow and reduced to visibility to one quarter mile or less. White-out conditions were observed at times.  A peak wind gust of 47 kt/54 mph was observed at the Deadhorse ASOS, and a peak wind gust of 56 kt/64 mph was observed at the Barter Island AWSS.  Once the blizzard conditions ended at Barter Island late on the afternoon of the 29th, the wind continued to frequently gust in excess of 50 mph, and near blizzard conditions continued at times through the early morning hours of the 30th. According to public accounts from Barter Island the day after the storm, conditions were not nearly as severe during this blizzard as they were during the blizzard in early January.  The snowdrifts were only about half as high as they were during the previous blizzard, and there were no known power outages.

A 996 mb low near Banks Island combined with a 1037 mb high across the southern Chukchi Sea to produce a period of blizzard conditions along the Beaufort Sea Coast.  Severe blizzard conditions were observed along the eastern Beaufort Sea Coast.  At Barter Island, the wind gusted to at least 63kt/72 mph and then power was lost at the AWSS. According to reports from the public in the area, the power went out for about 4 hours on the morning of the 2nd, and the visibility was reduced to zero at times for about 1 1/2 days.  Once the storm ended there were snowdrifts that were estimated between 10 and 12 feet high.||Along the Central Beaufort Sea coast, conditions were not as severe, but blizzard conditions were observed from the early morning hours on the 1st through the mid-morning hours on the 2nd.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow.  The wind gusted as high as 42kt/48 mph at the Deadhorse ASOS.

A 954 mb low in the Gulf of Alaska combined with a 1043 mb high across the Brooks Range to produce two periods of high winds at Unalakleet. The first occurred during the early morning hours on the 10th when there was a measured wind gust to 55 kt/63 mph at the Unalakleet AWOS.  The second period of high winds occurred during the late afternoon and evening hours on the 10th when there was a peak wind gust of 53kt/61 mph at the Unalakleet AWOS.

The first occurred during the early morning hours on the 10th when there was a measured wind gust to 55 kt/63 mph at the Unalakleet AWOS.  The second period of high winds occurred during the late afternoon and evening hours on the 10th when there was a peak wind gust of 53kt/61 mph at the Unalakleet AWOS.

An arctic high that was accompanied by an ample supply of cold air built in along the northern coast of Alaska.  Temperatures were observed as low as -36F at the Wainwright ASOS and -42F at the Atqasuk AWSS.  The cold temperatures combined with an east wind of 15 to 20 mph to produce wind chills as low as -66F at Wainwright and -74F at Atqasuk.  The wind chills were 60 below or lower from the morning hours on the 10th through shortly after midnight on the 11th.

A 968 mb low near the central Aleutians combined with a strong ridge of high pressure across eastern Russia to produce blizzard conditions at Gambell on Saint Lawrence Island as well as at Cape Romanzoff along the Yukon Delta Coast.  Heavy snow was observed at Mountain Village along the Yukon Delta.  Heavy snowfall was also observed at Shageluk in the lower Yukon Valley, and at Shaktoolik along the eastern Norton Sound.  A disturbance developed along a frontal boundary that extended from the low into Norton Sound, and produced heavy snowfall along parts of the Seward Peninsula. Eight inches of snow was observed at Buckland.  ||Zone 210: Snow fell at Buckland from about 10 pm AKST on the 4th and continued until about 6 pm AKST on the 5th.  Some of the snow fell heavily at times, and a total of 8 inches of snow was observed at Buckland. ||Zone 212: Heavy snow fell along parts of the eastern Norton Sound Coast.  It was estimated that 5 to 10 inches of snow fell at Shaktoolik.  The amount of snow that fell was nearly impossible to measure due to significant blowing and drifting snow.  Snow drifts of at least 4 feet were observed by the morning of the 5th as the storm ended. ||Zone 213: Blizzard conditions were observed at Gambell on Saint Lawrence Island from 4 am AKST until 2 pm AKST on the 4th.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow.  The wind at the Gambell AWOS gusted as high as 49kt/56 mph.||Zone 214: Heavy snowfall was observed at Mountain Village with a storm total of 8 to 9 inches. Blizzard conditions were observed at Cape Romanzof along the coast during the late morning and early afternoon hours on the 4th. The visibility was reduced to less than one quarter or a mile, and the wind gusted as high as 45kt/52 mph at the Cape Romanzof AWOS.||Zone 215: Heavy snowfall was observed at Shageluk with a storm total of approximately 6 inches.  The amount of snow that fell was difficult to measure due to extensive drifting of the snow that fell.

A 999 mb low several hundred miles north of Demarcation Point at 9 am AKST on the 14th gradually deepened to 985 mb by 9 am AKST on the 15th as the low very slowly lifted to the north. At the same time a weak ridge of high pressure built into the Brooks Range.  The pressure gradient between the high and low produced southwest winds of 15 to 25 mph.  The wind combined with temperatures of 30 to 35 below zero to produce wind chills as low as 68 below at Deadhorse.  Here are the lowest wind chills that were observed during this event:||Deadhorse ASOS: 68 below|Deadhorse Alpine Airport SAWRS: 68 below|Nuiqsut ASOS: 66 below|Kuparuk SAWRS: 66 below.

Cold arctic air from northwest Canada moved into to the northern panhandle behind an arctic cold front to the northern panhandle through the 12th. A deep low 955mb move into the southwest Gulf of Alaska from the north Pacific Ocean.  The front approached the coast of Alaska but dissipated as it was moving onshore. A strong overrunning snow event to the northern half of the panhandle combined with a strong north outflow winds from Canada resulted in a multiple day winter storm event for the northern panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gustavus coop observer reported a snow accumulation just under 6 inches the morning of the 11th. By the morning the 12th at 7 am AKST and additional 7 inches had fallen with another 6.2 by the morning of the 13th.  Storm total for the event in Gustavus was 18.9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic boundary lay across the far northern section of the  panhandle containing a pool of cold air in place.  A moderately strong low moved into the southwest gulf and then to the northwest gulf by the afternoon of the 16th.  The frontal bands moisture overran the cold arctic air allowing around a foot to snow to fall across the northern panhandle near the border.  Ahead of the front as it moved to the shore of the southern panhandle strong south winds spread in, the night of the 16th to the early morning hours of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thorne Bay coop observer reported that there were downed trees across power lines resulting in power outages overnight on 1/16. There were reports of very strong winds in mid Clarence Strait, gusts over 65kt, that would have been affecting exposed capes along the strait.

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather front associated with a strong low in the northern Pacific Ocean moved into the Gulf of Alaska from the south on the 27th and overnight into the morning of the 28th. The front moved onshore the southern panhandle the morning of the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on the NCEP surface observation map, a tight pressure gradient was oriented along the west coast of Prince of Wales Island, the evening of the 27th and into the morning of the 28th. This gradient is typically strong enough to produce gusts of 60 mph or more over western portion of the island.

This resulted in a burst of heavy snow in Haines in the morning hours of 1/7, before it warmed sufficiently to change the snow to liquid, however persistent cold air at the surface continued through the morning of 1/8, so the rain was freezing on the surface in Haines causing minor ice accumulations through 0800 AKST 1/8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Haines ASOS reported heavy snow began roughly around 0500 AKST on 1/7.  he coop observer in Haines reported an accumulation of 7.4 inches of snow at 0800 AKST on 1/7.  Freezing rain began at the Haines ASOS began at 1318 AKST and continued over night to roughly 0800 on 1/8.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Juneau airport and the NWS Forecast Office picked up nearly 8 inches (7.7 and 8.0 respectively) by midnight the night of the 11th. In Douglas a NWS employee reported 8 inches by 3pm AKST the 11th and a total of 13 inches by 10am the 12th. A coop in Tee harbor reported a snow fall of 7 inches on the 11th.  Coop sites located in colder valleys near Juneau Canyon Island reported 19.8 inches  (24 hour totals at 8am AKST 8.7 on 11th and 11.1 the 12th) and Annex Creek Power Station 20.0 inches (at 6 pm AKST 11.0 on the 11th and 9.0 inches on the 12th).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure dropped out of the high Arctic and deepened to 998 mb just off the coast of Banks Island.  The low combined with a strong ridge of high pressure that extended from northern Russia and into the Brooks Range to produce a very strong pressure gradient along the Beaufort Sea Coast.  High winds combined with falling snow to produce significant blowing and drifting snow and blizzard conditions.  ||Zone 203: Blizzard conditions were observed at Deadhorse from approximately 8 am on the morning of the 2nd through 2 am on the morning of the 3rd. A peak wind gust of 56 mph/49 kt was observed at the Deadhorse ASOS.||Zone 204: Blizzard conditions were observed at Barter Island from approximately 5 am on the morning of the 2nd through 5 am on the morning of the 3rd.  The blizzard was more severe at Barter Island than it was further to the west along the central Beaufort Sea Coast.  A peak wind gust of 71 mph/62kt was observed at the Barter Island AWSS.  According to public accounts, there were minor power outages during the storm and the school was closed for two days.  The drifting snow was likely significant as the school remained closed the day after the blizzard ended.

A stationary 1056 mb high 600 miles north of Wrangel Island combined with several weak waves of low pressure that tracked north along the west coast of Alaska to produce a strong pressure gradient along the Arctic coast.  An east wind of 25 to 40 mph produced significant blowing and drifting snow.  The moisture that was associated with the low also produced falling snow along much of the western and northern Arctic coast.  Blizzard conditions were observed, with the visibility frequently less than one quarter mile. White-out conditions were observed at times. The worst conditions were observed along the western Arctic coast where the blizzard conditions persisted for 48 hours. ||Zone 201: According to local law enforcement in the village of Atquasuk snow drifts of 12 to 16 feet were observed when the blizzard ended.  There were public accounts of snow drifts that were estimated to be about 5 feet in the village of Wainwright. A peak wind gust of 44 kt/51 mph was observed at the Wainwright ASOS.||Zone 202: A peak gust of 38kt/44 mph was observed during this event at the Barrow ASOS.||Zone 203: A peak wind gust of 40kt/46 mph was observed during this event at the Deadhorse ASOS.

An intense North Pacific storm moved across the Bering Sea north of the Aleutian Islands. As it passed the central Aleutians, hurricane force gusts to 75 mph were observed at Adak. This storm produced strong wind and snow in the Pribilof Islands resulting in a blizzard. As the storm moved east, strong north wind and snow around the west side of the low resulted in blizzard conditions along the western Alaska Peninsula.

A 964 mb low across the eastern Aleutians combined with a moderate ridge of high pressure across the Arctic Ocean and Siberia to produce blizzard conditions at Gambell on Saint Lawrence Island from the evening of the 9th through the early evening of the 10th.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less, with white-out conditions observed at times.  A northeast wind of 30 to 40 mph was observed during the event, with a peak wind gust at the Gambell AWOS of 41kt/47 mph. The anemometer was only functional intermittently during the evening of the 9th into the early morning of the 10th, and it is possible that the peak wind gusts may have been a little stronger.

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low pressure system developed off the Queen Charlotte Islands on Friday, February 5th. This caused a very strong pressure gradient along the west side of Prince of Wales Island around midday Friday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter at Edna Bay observed a 45 MPH gust at 0900 2/5. There was a massive observation outage during that time, but pressure gradients from NCEP surface analysis were very strong. Lincoln Rock, a marine observation in Clarence Strait, had a peak wind of 52 KT at 1000 AKST on 2/5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense North Pacific storm moved along the Aleutian Islands producing strong wind and snow resulting in blizzard conditions from the central Aleutians to the Alaska Peninsula, north to the Pribilof Islands.

An intense North Pacific storm moved along the Aleutian Islands producing strong wind and snow resulting in blizzard conditions from the central Aleutians to the Alaska Peninsula, north to the Pribilof Islands.

CZ_NAME: CAPE DECISION TO SALISBURY SOUND COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms kept on slamming SE Alaska on 10/12. On Tue 10/12 a 972 MB Hurricane Force Center made landfall on Prince of Wales Island in the morning then recurved to Cape Spencer in the afternoon on 10/12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sitka Airport measured gusts to 77 mph around noon on 10/12. Power outages and downed trees were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On 12/3 arctic high pressure over the Yukon gave way to a front moving over the area from the south. This front was associated with a storm in the Bering Sea. Warm moist air moved over the remaining cold air at the surface. Snow changed rapidly to rain after the front passed later on 12/3 and then as a second mass of cold air moved through changed back to snow again.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Haines customs measured 4.5 inches of new snowfall at 0700 AKST on 12/4 and 5.0 inches new snow at 0700 AKST on 12/5. By analysis 6 inches fell during 12 hours ending at 1300 AKST on 12/4.

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong hurricane force low developed off the Queen Charlotte Islands on Friday evening 2/12 down to 952 MB. The storm center then moved very slowly toward the north while slightly weakening.  A very strong associated front moved across Prince of Wales Island on Saturday afternoon, 2/13.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Edna Bay spotter observed 45 MPH winds on Saturday 2/13. Hyder AWOS observed gusts to 44 MPH and Lincoln Rock (marine observation) had gusts to 60 MPH on the evening of 2/13. NCEP analysis showed extreme surface pressure gradients that had to cause headland winds in excess of 80 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of 12/29 arctic air was over the northern Panhandle. A strong warm front was approaching from the south associated with as storm center all the way over at the eastern Aleutians. warm over running snow continued into the morning of the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Skagway customs measured 9 inches of new snowfall by 0700 on the 31st. The heaviest snow fell during the night of the 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of 12/29 arctic air was over the northern Panhandle. A strong warm front was approaching from the south associated with as storm center all the way over at the eastern Aleutians. warm over running snow continued into the morning of the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WSO Yakutat measured 6.4 inches of new snow on 12/29 followed by 4.3 inches on 12/30. Snow changed to rain on the evening of 12/30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam formed in front of the village of Buckland along the Buckland River.  The ice jam caused the water levels to rise rapidly during the afternoon hours of the 20th.  Many roads were flooded, and the village dump was submerged.  The ice knocked down a power pole late on the evening of the 20th or early on the morning of the 21st, and cut power to the airport.  The ice jam finally released around 2200AKST on the 21st, and the water level rapidly dropped about 6 feet ending the flooding.  Three roads were washed out with damage to 2 culverts.  Total damage was estimated at $135,000.

CZ_NAME: JUNEAU BOROUGH AND NORTHERN ADMIRALTY ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm moist air aloft moved over the cooler temperatures in SE Alaska on 3/12 which caused heavy snowfall for most of the northern sections. This snow was followed by a rapidly developing storm moving up from the SW over the Gulf of Alaska on 3/12 and 3/13. A strong front associated with this front moved onto the coast on 3/13.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A moderate snowstorm hit Juneau on 3/12 into 3/13. A trained spotter observed 6.5 inches of new snowfall for 12 hours ending at 1900 AKST on 3/12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam near Ruby caused the water levels on the Yukon River to rise significantly on the 10th, and flood stage was exceeded by 0500AKST on the 11th.  The water level crested at 41.5 feet at Ruby 1000AKST on the 11th, and remained above flood stage until 2100AKST on the 13th.  The stage of 41.5 feet was the highest on record at Ruby.  The village of Ruby sits on a hill above the Yukon River, and damages were limited to two fish camps and two cabins that were inundated with 3 to 6 feet of water.  Two additional cabins were damaged by ice. The cabins were located on the Novi River, with the exception of one fish camp on the Yukon River near Ruby.
Hail of 0.75 in diameter was observed by a trained weather spotter 2 miles south of Fox.

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent storm force low moved into the Gulf of Alaska on 3/26. A series of strong fronts moved onto the SE Alaska coast on 3/27 and 3/28 causing strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hydaburg AWOS observed a gust to 58 MPH at 1816 AKST on 3/26 and gusts to 71 MPH at 1416 on 3/27. Gusts in the 60s were observed into the early morning hours of 3/28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Turquoise Lake Fire is believed to be a human caused fire that started on May 18th.  The fire grew rapidly and burned a total of 91,885 acres.  The fire consumed the Farewell Lake Lodge including four cabins and several outbuildings at approximately 3 pm AKST on the 28th.  Several people had to be evacuated from the property, and damage estimates are approximately 1.5 million dollars.

A hurricane force low down to 956 MB developed near Dutch Harbor on 3/4 and was supported by very cold air aloft. This storm center tracked to South Central AK on 3/5 and set the North Pacific storm track from the SW into the Panhandle due to very persistent winds aloft. A multitude of fronts and developing waves moved from the Gulf of Alaska into SE Alaska through 3/10 bringing both snow and high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Skagway Customs received 10.0 inches new snowfall for 24 hours ending at 1600 AKST on 3/6 plus 3 more inches new snow during the evening. Strong winds and low visibility were also reported in this storm. See blizzard on the previous event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hurricane force low down to 956 MB developed near Dutch Harbor on 3/4 and was supported by very cold air aloft. This storm center tracked to South Central AK on 3/5 and set the North Pacific storm track from the SW into the Panhandle due to very persistent winds aloft. A multitude of fronts and developing waves moved from the Gulf of Alaska into SE Alaska through 3/10 bringing both snow and high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Haines area was hit hard by this winter storm. On the afternoon of 3/5 near white-out conditions were reported by several spotters, Snow accumulated to 8.5 inches by 0700 AKST on 3/6. Numerous wind gusts to 40 MPH were observed though 3/6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hurricane force low down to 956 MB developed near Dutch Harbor on 3/4 and was supported by very cold air aloft. A multitude of fronts and developing waves moved from the Gulf of Alaska into SE Alaska through 3/10 bringing both snow and high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of 3/5 Skagway Airport had visibilities below 2 miles in snow. |Frequent gusts above 35 MPH were also observed. A trained spotter up the Klondike Highway at 1400 AKST observed blowing snow and it is likely that white out conditions occurred further up the highway. Several reports of 1/4 mile in snow were observed on the morning of 3/6. More gusts in the 50 MPH range occurred in the afternoon at which point the snow then changed to rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hail reported by a Cooperative Observer, located just east of Fort Wainwright near Badger Road. The observer estimated a size of up to 1.25 inches, but no damage was reported to property or to vehicles parked outside. No other supporting reports nearby.  Observer also reported soft mushy character to the hail accumulation, so suspect the larger size estimate may have come from the freezing together of several stones.  NWS adjusted diameter size downward to an estimated 0.75 inches.
A National Weather Service IMET observed hail up to 3/4 inch in diameter near Tok.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm moist air aloft moved over the cooler temperatures in SE Alaska on 3/12 which caused heavy snowfall for most of the northern sections. This snow was followed by a rapidly developing storm moving up from the SW over the Gulf of Alaska on 3/12 and 3/13. A strong front associated with this front moved onto the coast on 3/13.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The downtown Haines COOP observer measured 11.9 inches of new snow for 24 hours ending at 0800 AKST on 3/13. Haines Customs measured 9.0 inches new snow on 3/13 and 17.5 new on 3/14.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hidden Falls measured 7.0 inches of new snow for 12 hours ending at 0700 AKST 3/13. |Most of this snow fell on the evening of 3/12. Sitka had considerably less snow.

CZ_NAME: WRN TANANA VLY WRN YUKON VLY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm remained nearly stationary across the Elliot Highway between mile markers 70 and 85.  The storm produced flash flooding at mile marker 83.  Doppler radar estimated that between 4 and 5 inches of rain fell. The highway was washed out with significant debris across the highway.  It was estimated by the DOT that the water was likely between 1 and 2 feet deep when the flash flood occurred. A motorist crossed this section of the highway a few hours after the heavy rain ended, and observed that water and debris were still gushing across the highway from a culvert along the side of the road.

Just 10 days after a record flood impacted the Fortymile River and its tributaries another flood impacted the area.  Water levels returned to pre-flood levels, however, occasional showers and saturated soils resulted in more significant rises than would normally be observed.  Widespread rain fell across the Fortymile basin on the 22nd into the 23rd and produced between 1 and 2 inches of rain.  A National Weather Service observer at the Walker Fork of the Fortymile River reported a stage only about one half foot lower than the previous flood.  The Walker Fork campground was inundated with water and water was once again flowing across the Taylor Highway just north of the campground along with washouts about one mile north of the campground.  A National Weather Service observer at O'Brien Creek reported a rise of nearly one foot per hour and a crest on the 23rd that was very close to the record crest observed on the 12th.  The main channel of the Fortymile River at the Taylor Highway bridge crested approximately 7 feet below the level seen on the 12th, but still experienced a rise of nearly 13 feet. The State of Alaska declared the event a disaster with an estimate repair cost of $6 million.

CZ_NAME: WRN TANANA VLY WRN YUKON VLY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms crossed the Tanana area late on the evening of the 3rd.  A wind gust to 58 mph was observed at the Tanana ASOS at 2116AKST.  The high winds associated with the thunderstorm produced wind damage.  A total of 22 trees, some with a trunk diameter over 2 feet were blown over.  One residential powerline fell, with associated cable TV lines.  A roof was blown off of a shed as well as a small smokehouse.  Numerous boats along the Yukon River were blown up on the shore, and some of them had minor damage.

A 990 mb low passed between Wrangel Island and the northwest coast of Alaska on the 22nd. A south wind of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 40 mph occurred ahead of the low and its associated cold front. Tides of 1 to 2 feet above normal combined with high surf to produce minor beach erosion in the vicinity of Kivalina.   Some rocks were lost along the beach, but there was no other known property damage.

A one day record rainfall of 1.22 inches in Anchorage resulted in local streams rising to near flood stage. Chester Creek recorded a stage of 19.58, 0.92 feet below flood stage, but withing the action stage. Local bike and walking paths experience minor overflow in low lying portions of the trails.

A strong front moved into south central Alaska August 16th. Strong wind in advance of the front blew from Potter Marsh and McHugh Creek to the Anchorage Hillside. The peak measured wind was 72 mph at McHugh Creek. Power outages occurred in the Anchorage area and small limbs broke and potted plants were blown around.

An area of low pressure moved out of the Gulf of Alaska and to the Alaska-Yukon border on the 11th.  As the low moved southeast and into the southern Yukon moisture wrapped around the low and produced heavy rainfall across the Fortymile County.  Rainfall amounts of 3.2 inches were observed at Chicken and O'Brien Creek, which is over one quarter of the average annual rainfall in just a little over one day.|The heavy rain caused the Fortymile River at the Taylor Highway bridge to rise 22 feet and the river crested at 94.5 feet.  This surpassed the previous record of 92.4 ft on May 3, 1979 and may have surpassed the all-time record observed in 1964. Unfortunately, there are no records of the peak crest in 1964.  The crest observed on O'Brien Creek which is a tributary of the Fortymile River was the highest on record.   Due to the areal extent and distribution of the rainfall over the region and the resulting flood damage, this suggests that the flood was likely one of the highest floods on record for the Fortymile River basin and its tributaries. |The heavy rain washed out parts of the Taylor Highway and stranded 20 to 30 people.  Seven portions of the road were completely washed out for several hundred feet, and multiple mud slides took out parts of the road. A U.S Customs Agent was missing and presumed dead after searchers found his U.S. Government Vehicle in O'Brien Creek. The Alaska State Troopers stated the driver evidently failed to negotiate a corner and his vehicle went down a 200-foot embankment and ended up i the creek.

An area of low pressure moved out of the Gulf of Alaska and into the eastern interior of Alaska on August 6th.  The low stalled out near the Alaska-Yukon border north of Eagle and produced heavy rainfall across parts of the Fortymile River Basin as well as American Creek.  One and one half to two and one half inches of rain fell during a 24 hour period from Chicken northward to Eagle.  The National Weather Service observer at O'Brien Creek recorded a peak stage of 71.3 feet, which tied the record stage set back on July 11th, 2010.  It is likely that American Creek, which is located just to the north and parallels the Taylor Highway, experienced a similar record peak stage.  The heavy rain fell north of the southern portion of the Fortymile basin, and the main stem of the Fortymile River at the Taylor Highway crested about 11 feet below the record stage of July 12th.  The heavy rainfall produced numerous rock slides and washouts north of Chicken.

A 958 mb low in the northern Gulf of Alaska early on the morning of the 4th rapidly moved into interior Alaska during the afternoon and weakened to 976 mb.  A strong difference in pressure between the low in the interior and a moderate ridge of high pressure off the arctic coast produced blizzard conditions at Atqasuk during the afternoon of the 4th.  A northeast wind of 35 to 45 mph produced significant blowing and drifting snow and reduced the visibility to zero at times.  Conditions improved during the early evening as the strong wind diminished.

A strong low moved into the Gulf of Alaska. This storm, coupled with high pressure over interior Alaska produced strong north winds across the region and through the channeled terrain of south central Alaska. Over 10,000 people lost electric power in the south central region as a result of the high wind. The strongest wind observed was a 78 mph gust in the Palmer Wasilla area. This strong wind event occurred early in the fall while trees still had leaves on them. This resulted in an uncharacteristically high number of trees being blown down, some of which fell across power-lines causing the unusually high number of power outages. Based upon insurance company information, it is estimated that $500,000 of damage occurred from this storm in the Matanuska Valley to the Anchorage area.

A strong low that was the remnants of an old tropical system produced warning level winds in and near Adak, Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong low in the Bering Sea brought warning level winds to Adak, Alaska.

An intensifying low moved south across the Arctic Ocean and deepened to 990 millibars about 350 miles north of Barrow by the morning of the 25th.  Sea ice was near seasonal minimums and there was several hundred miles of open water to the northwest of Barrow.  A northwest wind around the back side of the low increased to sustained speeds of 20 to 30 mph with gusts to 40 mph.  The strong wind caused seas to build to an estimated 5 to 10 feet that broke along the beach.  Seas were estimated to be as high as 15 feet offshore.  Although the storm surge was estimated to be less than 1 foot, the high seas did cause some minor flooding around Barrow.  The road that runs parallel to the beach was closed due to minor flooding.  Minor flooding was also reported at the public playground and in low lying areas near the Top of the World Hotel.  The boat ramp was also reported to be flooding.  No property damage was reported during the storm.  The city built up berms that were estimated to be 6 feet high and the berms likely helped to mitigate flood damage.

A rapidly developing storm force low moved into the eastern Gulf of Alaska on the morning of Friday, September 24th. The storm center developed to 953 MB around 9 AM AKST and an associated, very strong front moved onto the south coast of the panhandle that Friday afternoon. Winds in excess 75 MPH were observed. After the front passed there was a significant outbreak of maritime thunderstorms over the coastal waters with a waterspout conditions observed on radar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ketchikan airport observed gusts to 64 MPH at 0830 AKST on Friday 9/24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely strong front associated with a Gulf storm brought hurricane force winds to SE Alaska on 10/5. Moisture ahead of this front brought brief heavy snow to the Chilkat Highway. Trees down and power outages were the norm with this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusts to 60 MPH were routine for the Ketchikan area on the morning of 10/5. Power outages were caused due to tree branches down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 975 mb low passed a few hundred miles south of Saint Lawrence Island during the morning of the 3rd.  The low combined with a 1032 mb high across the Chukchi Sea to produce strong winds and blizzard conditions at Gambell from late on the evening of the 2nd through the early morning hours on the 3rd.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less.  Northeast winds were sustained at 35 to 43 mph with a peak wind gust of 46 kt (53 mph).

A 1006 mb low in the Beaufort Sea a couple hundred miles offshore of the eastern Beaufort Sea Coast combined with a strong ridge of high pressure building into Northwest Alaska to produce strong winds and blizzard conditions at Barter Island.  Frequent blizzard conditions were observed at the Barter Island AWSS from 0541AKST on the 14th through 0038AKST on the 15th.  A peak wind gust of 59 mph (51 kt) was observed at the Barter Island AWSS during the event.

An upper level low developed in the Norton Sound during the afternoon of the 19th and produced the first significant snowfall of the season across the Middle Yukon Valley.  The low caused a strong south flow to develop and transported moisture into the area.  Temperatures were just cold enough to support snow and some of the snow fell heavily at times.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 8 inches across the area. The cooperative observer in Galena observed a total of 8 inches of snow and there were public reports from Huslia of 7 to 8 inches of snow. Six inches of snow was observed by the public at Ruby through 1100AKST on the 20th, but additional accumulating snow likely occurred during the afternoon and evening hours.

A few periods of strong north wind at Point Hope during the last week of the month produced significant beach erosion at Point Hope.  A total of 12 feet of beach along the north shore of the village was lost. At times the north wind gusted as high as |45 kt (52 mph) at the Point Hope AWOS.

A storm in the Bering Sea resulted in strong warm and moist southerly fetch off the North Pacific. This warm moist air flowed over entrenched cold air over south central Alaska resulted in freezing rain that deposited over one quarter inch of ice across portions of south central Alaska. Freezing rain below warning criteria also fell across the Copper River Basin and the western Kenai Peninsula and isolated portions of northern Prince William Sound.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous forecast office employees observed the freezing rain and heavy ice accumulation.

CZ_NAME: SALISBURY SOUND TO CAPE FAIRWEATHER COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms kept on slamming SE Alaska on 10/12. On Tue 10/12 a 972 MB Hurricane Force Center made landfall on Prince of Wales Island in the morning then recurved to Cape Spencer in the afternoon on 10/12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Land stations were not available to measure this wind storm. Cape Spencer measured a peak wind of 86 KT (103 MPH) at 1900 AKST on 10/12. Gusts to 70 MPH would have occurred over land around this time.

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hydaburg ASOS measued gusts to 61 KT at 0736 AKST on 10/12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms kept on slamming SE Alaska on 10/12. On Tue 10/12 a 972 MB Hurricane Force Center made landfall on Prince of Wales Island in the morning then recurved to Cape Spencer in the afternoon on 10/12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Ketchikan observed gusts to 70 MPH at 1045 AKST on 10/12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms kept on slamming SE Alaska on 10/12. On Tue 10/12 a 972 MB Hurricane Force Center made landfall on Prince of Wales Island in the morning then recurved to Cape Spencer in the afternoon on 10/12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although land statations did not measure winds above 50 MPH, Sister Island measured a gust to 67 KT at noon on 10/12. Headlands along Icy Strait easily had 70 MPH gusts.

CZ_NAME: JUNEAU BOROUGH AND NORTHERN ADMIRALTY ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms kept on slamming SE Alaska on 10/12. On Tue 10/12 a 972 MB Hurricane Force Center made landfall on Prince of Wales Island in the morning then recurved to Cape Spencer in the afternoon on 10/12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Juneau Airport observed gusts to 55 KT at 1653 AKDT on 10/12 and a peak of 59 KT at 1353 AKDT. This is very strong for the airport. Other observations were in the 60s (MPH). Trees were downed and power outages were frequent and sustained.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm center developed just off the Queen Charlotte Islands on the afternoon of 10/9. The center recurved to the NW over the Panhandle causing hurricane force winds, with many, many power outages and downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain accompanied the extreme hurricane force winds that slammed the Ketchikan area. Boats went adrift from moorings. Power was out for hours. Numerous trees were downed due to the wind. 110 KT measured in the Thomas Boat Basin. The airport only had gusts to 61 KT. Trees bent over as never before... from a spotter report.

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After the storm on 10/12, a secondary low developed on the front and moved into Dixon Entrance on the afternoon of the 13th. This second low intensified as it recurved to Cape Spencer and caused hurricane force winds on 10/14 for the southern coast. Widespread power outages and trees were downed for the southern Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hydaburg AWOS measured gusts to 78 MPH at 2056 AKST on 10/13. Plenty of gusts were in 50s and 60s until midnight.

CZ_NAME: INNER CHANNELS FROM KUPREANOF ISLAND TO ETOLIN ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After the storm on 10/12, a secondary low developed on the front and moved into Dixon Entrance on the afternoon of the 13th. This second low intensified as it recurved to Cape Spencer and caused hurricane force winds on 10/14 for the southern coast. Widespread power outages and trees were downed for the southern Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cape Decision light observed 69 KT at 2100 on 10/13. No land stations observed more than 50 MPH, but headlands in the Inner Channels had gusts to 70 MPH due to the extreme pressure gradients with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After the storm on 10/12, a secondary low developed on the front and moved into Dixon Entrance on the afternoon of the 13th. This second low intensified as it recurved to Cape Spencer and caused hurricane force winds on 10/14 for the southern coast. Widespread power outages and trees were downed for the southern Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ketchikan airport ASOS measured gusts to 60 KT through midnight on 10/13-14. A nearby marine observation at Lincoln Rock had gusts in excess of 100 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 960 MB low in the Bering Sea created strong west and northwest winds along the Alaska Peninsula. The channeled terrain near King Cove caused gusts that peaked at 78 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 960 mb low in the Bering Sea caused winds in King Cove, Alaska to gust over warning levels for 13 hours. The peak gust during that time was measured at 78 mph. No damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely strong front associated with a Gulf storm brought hurricane force winds to SE Alaska on 10/5. Moisture ahead of this front brought brief heavy snow to the Chilkat Highway. Trees down and power outages were the norm with this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter on the Chilikat Peninsula measured 6 inches of new snow in 12 hours ending at 0930 AKST on 10/5. Haines customs measured 4.5 inches of new snow at 0700 AKST on the morning of 10/5 with continuing snowfall. This snowstorm was important in that it was the first one of the year.

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely strong front associated with a Gulf storm brought hurricane force winds to SE Alaska on 10/5. Moisture ahead of this front brought brief heavy snow to the Chilkat Highway. Trees down and power outages were the norm with this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hydaburg AWOS measured gusts in the 50s including a gust to 58 KT at 5 AM AKST on 10/5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure remained over the NW Territories as a storm force low moved into the central Gulf of Alaska. A strong associated front moved onto the coast of SE Alaska on the night of 11/29. This system brought heavy snow to northern Lynn Canal and high winds to the coastal areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Haines Customs measured 14 inches of new snowfall for 24 hours ending at 0700 AKST on 11/29. Various amounts were measured around the town of Haines with some rain mixed in. Winds to 30 mph were observed with poor visibility on the Chilkat highway. Near white out conditions occurred at times along the highway. This event lasted around two days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure remained over the NW Territories as a storm force low moved into the central Gulf of Alaska. A strong associated front moved onto the coast of SE Alaska on the night of 11/29. This system brought heavy snow to northern Lynn Canal and high winds to the coastal areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began at Skagway airport around 0300 AKST on 11/28. It snowed there continuously until 0400 on 11/29. 8 to 10 inches fell on the Klondike Highway between the night of 11/28 and 0730 AKST on 11/29.  10 inches fell in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense Gulf of Alaska storm produced strong northwest wind and snow across Kodiak resulting in blizzard conditions. This same storm also resulted in strong northwest wind across the Alaska Peninsula.

A strong Gulf of Alaska storm coupled with deep cold arctic air over interior Alaska produced strong north gap winds through the Chugach Mountains. Peak wind gusts of 94 mph were observed at the Valdez airport during this event.

An intense Gulf of Alaska storm produced strong northwest wind and snow across Kodiak resulting in blizzard conditions. This same storm also resulted in strong northwest wind across the Alaska Peninsula.

A 950 mb low in the northern Bering Sea pushed a strong occluded front across the Nulato Hills.  The front combined with southerly flow aloft to produce heavy snowfall across parts of the Nulato Hills. The cooperative weather observer in Kaltag measured 10 inches of snow from 1400AKST on the 27th through 1400AKST on the 28th.  The snow was heavy and wet with temperatures near freezing the snow compacted down to approximately 6 inches of snow on the ground by the time the snow ended during the afternoon of the 28th.

A strong storm in the Gulf of Alaska combined with deep cold arctic air and high pressure over interior Alaska resulted in strong north gap winds across south central Alaska. Along with the strong wind, low temperatures resulted in low wind chills across much of the south central and southeast mainland regions of Alaska. The peak measured wind was 87 mph in the Wasilla area. Gusts very likely reached around 100 mph during this event based upon the damage and power outages associated with this event in the Palmer and Wasilla area. Peak wind gust were measured to 91 mph through Thompson Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust are estimated for this area based upon the mesonet sites located at Strawberry Reef and the Million Dollar bridge near the Copper River delta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure across the Bering Sea combined with a trough of low pressure offshore of the eastern Beaufort Sea to produce a period of strong west wind along the eastern Beaufort Sea coast during the late morning and early afternoon hours of the 15th.  The strong wind combined with falling snow to produce a period of blizzard conditions.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile.  Peak wind speeds of 41kt (47 mph) were observed at the Barter Island AWSS.

The combination of a strong 1050 mb high across the southern interior of Alaska and a 992 mb low that moved from west to east across the Arctic Ocean and passed about 400 miles north of Barrow during the afternoon of the 17th produced snow and strong winds and led to a period of blizzard conditions in zones 201 through 206 on the 17th.  The following are the conditions that were observed across the area:||Zone 201: Blizzard conditions were observed at Atqasuk during the late morning and afternoon hours on the 17th.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile in snow and blowing snow.  A peak wind gust of 43 kt/49 mph was observed at the Atqasuk AWSS.  Wind gusts as high as 62kt (71 mph) were observed at the Cape Lisburne AWOS. ||Zone 202: Blizzard conditions were observed at Barrow during the morning hours on the 17th.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile in snow and blowing snow.  A peak wind gust of 42kt (48 mph) was observed at the Barrow ASOS.  ||Zone 203: Blizzard conditions were observed at times during the day on the 17th along the central Beaufort Sea Coast.  The visibility at Nuiqsut was reduced to one quarter mile at times in snow and blowing snow during the afternoon and evening hours.  A peak wind gust of 53kt (61 mph) was observed at the Nuiqsut ASOS.  Blizzard conditions were observed Deadhorse during the afternoon and evening on the 17th.  A peak wind gust of 51 kt (59 mph) was observed at the Deadhorse ASOS.  Blizzard conditions were also observed at times at Kuparuk and Alpine.  Peak wind gusts of 55 kt (63 mph) were observed at Kuparuk and 42kt (48 mph) at the Alpine SAWRS. ||Zone 204: Blizzard conditions were observed during the afternoon and evening hours of the 17th at Barter Island.  Although the AWSS only reported a short period of one quarter mile visibility, calls to the fire station in Barter Island indicated that the visibility was frequently one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow. A peak wind gust of 58 kt (67 mph) was observed at the Barter Island AWSS. ||Zone 205: Blizzard conditions likely occurred along the coastal plain in Zone 205 during the late morning and early afternoon hours on the 17th.  Due to limited observations in the area that blizzard conditions were based on the combination of a very strong pressure gradient and the blizzard conditions that were observed at Atqasuk which was the closest available observation platform. ||Zone 206: Blizzard conditions likely occurred on the coastal plain based on reports from the DOT of 40 to 50 mph wind and blowing and drifting snow reducing the visibility to near zero.  The visibility at Anaktuvuk Pass only briefly dropped to one quarter mile, but the visibilities were likely much lower along the coastal plain.
